title: God's Lamb
speaker: Pastor Chris Lensch
source_files: God’s Lamb - GUEST SPEAKER_ Pastor Chris Lensch - John 1.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Who Is This Lamb? (John 1:29-34)
subpoints: John the Baptist’s great proclamation: “Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world” (v. 29)
All Jewish listeners knew the language of sacrifice: the lamb that bears sin in the place of the guilty
The passage closes with John’s even greater claim: this is the Son of God (v. 34)
Both identities—Lamb and Son of God—are essential: the Lamb must be divine to have infinite worth

outline.title: How John Knew: The Sign of the Spirit
subpoints: John says twice: “I did not know Him” (vv. 31, 33)—despite being relatives
God gave John a sign: the Spirit descending and remaining on the one to be baptized
The Holy Spirit’s descent at Jesus’ baptism anoints Him for His priestly ministry
The same Spirit who gave the sign to John illumines believers today (1 Corinthians 2:11-12)

outline.title: The Lamb as High Priest: The Theology of Hebrews
subpoints: The baptism of Jesus publicly inaugurates His ministry in the office of the great High Priest
Hebrews 7:24-27: He offered Himself once for all—He is both the priest and the sacrifice
Hebrews 10:4: a body was prepared for Him, the necessary human body to represent and substitute for humanity
Jesus is without blemish—a perfect man and more: the divine Son in whose death infinite worth resides

outline.title: John the Baptist’s End: Assurance in Prison (Matthew 11)
subpoints: John dies in Herod’s prison, sending disciples to ask: “Are you the one?”
John may be doubting himself rather than Jesus—expecting greater things than a quiet ministry
Jesus sends back the evidence of His works: the deaf hear, the lame walk, the poor hear the gospel
In the same way, God gives us assurance through answered prayer, unexpected friendships, and surprising conversions

outline.title: The Lamb in Revelation: Worthy, Wrathful, and Reigning
subpoints: Revelation 5: John expects a lion but sees a Lamb—slain yet standing, holding authority to open the scroll of judgment
The Lamb is worthy because He was slain and redeemed a people from every tribe, tongue, and nation (vv. 9-12)
Revelation 6:16-17: the wrath of the Lamb—a rejected sacrifice is not treated with contempt without consequence
Revelation 19: two suppers—the marriage supper of the Lamb (for the redeemed) and the supper of God’s judgment (for the rebels)
Blessed are those invited to the marriage feast; those not found there will face the other supper

outline.title: Conclusion: Hope in the Risen Lamb
subpoints: Without the Lamb who was slain, we are like John the Baptist wasting in prison—we all face death
With the risen Christ, the risen Lamb who is still in control, we have hope now and hope for eternity
The redeemed will reign with Him—an active, holy, priestly life in the presence of God (Revelation 5:10)
Add your voice to the angels: “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain”

summary:
In this guest sermon, Pastor Chris Lensch opens in Revelation 5:6-14 before moving to John 1 to trace the title “Lamb of God” from its first prophetic announcement through to its final cosmic fulfillment. John the Baptist’s declaration—“Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world” (John 1:29)—would have been immediately understood by his Jewish audience as temple language: the lamb that is brought to God as a confession of debt and guilt. But the passage escalates, closing with John’s greatest claim: this is the Son of God (v. 34). Both identities together explain why Christ’s sacrifice has infinite and universal efficacy.

Pastor Lensch explores the paradox that John twice says he “did not know” Jesus despite them being relatives. God gave John a specific sign: the Spirit descending and remaining on the one to be baptized. This anointing at the Jordan River publicly inaugurates Jesus’ high-priestly ministry. Drawing on Hebrews 7 and 10, the sermon develops the theology of Christ as the Great High Priest who does not offer daily sacrifices for His own sins and then for the people’s, but who offered Himself once for all—being simultaneously the priest and the sacrifice, the offerer and the offering. The perfect human body prepared for Him was necessary to represent and substitute for humanity; His divine nature gave that offering its infinite worth.

The sermon pauses to consider John the Baptist’s end: imprisoned by Herod, he sends disciples to ask whether Jesus is really the one. The pastor reads this not as doubt about Jesus but as confusion about unfulfilled expectations—John seems to have expected more dramatic deliverance. Jesus’ answer is the evidence of His messianic works. This becomes an encouragement to believers who doubt in their own dark seasons: God continues to give assurance through answered prayer, unexpected friendship in times of need, and surprising conversions.

The final section surveys the Lamb in Revelation. In chapter five, John weeps expecting a lion—the warrior Messiah—but instead sees a Lamb, slain yet standing at the center of the throne. This Lamb is given the scroll of judgment because He purchased people from every nation by His blood. Chapter six introduces “the wrath of the Lamb”—a piercing image: a lamb is the most harmless of animals, yet those who have spurned this sacrifice will not escape its judgment. Chapter 19 presents two suppers: the marriage supper of the Lamb for the redeemed, and the terrible feast of judgment for the rebels. Blessed, the text declares, are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Revelation 5:6-14

If you're able to remain standing and can join me for the reading of Revelation chapter 5 verses 6 through 14.

*And I looked, and behold, in the midst of the throne and of the four living creatures and in the midst of the elders stood a Lamb as though it had been slain, having seven horns and seven eyes which are the seven Spirits of God sent out into all the earth. Then He came and took the scroll out of the right hand of Him who sat on the throne. Now when He had taken the scroll, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb, each having a harp and golden bowls full of incense which are the prayers of the saints. And they sang a new song saying, "You are worthy to take the scroll and to open its seals, for You were slain and have redeemed us to God by Your blood out of every tribe and tongue and people and nation, and have made us kings and priests to our God, and we shall reign on the earth." Then I looked, and I heard the voice of many angels around the throne, the living creatures and the elders, and the number of them was ten thousand times ten thousand and thousands of thousands, saying with a loud voice, "Worthy is the Lamb who is slain to receive power and riches and wisdom and strength and honor and glory and blessing." And every creature which is in heaven and on earth and under the earth and such as are in the sea and all that are in them I heard saying, "Blessing and honor and glory and power be to Him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb forever and ever." Then the four living creatures said "Amen" and the twenty-four elders fell down and worshiped Him who lives forever and ever.*

This is the word of the Lord. Be seated.

I'll be referencing that Revelation chapter five passage toward the end of the message, but to begin with I'd like us to turn to John chapter one, where the Lamb is introduced by His herald, John the Baptist.

## John the Baptist Introduces Jesus: John 1:29-34

John the Baptist had his own disciples. And he says in John one, verse 29:

*The next day John saw Jesus coming toward him and said, "Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world! This is He of whom I said, 'After me comes a Man who has preferred before me, for He was before me.' I did not know Him, but that He should be revealed to Israel, therefore I came baptizing with water." And John bore witness saying, "I saw the Spirit descending from heaven like a dove, and He remained upon Him. I did not know Him, but He who sent me to baptize with water said to me, 'Upon whom you see the Spirit descending and remaining on Him, this is He who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.' And I have seen and testified that this is the Son of God."*

So the passage opens with the Lamb, and everybody who was Jewish knew about sacrifices in the temple—how you bring a gift to God. You bring a lamb to confess that you are a debtor to God for your life. You take something innocent and undefiled and you bring that as the gift. They would lay their hands upon the lamb and say: take away my sins. I recognize that I'm a sinner. I'm in debt to God for the life that He gives to me.

But this passage we read closes with: behold him, and he is the very Son of God. He's God's Lamb, but he's also God's Son. And a lot of theology of identity going on with just who is this Jesus of Nazareth.

There was a recent TV show, I think it's called the Masked Singer. One of their singers who was masked—when they took the mask off, it was Rudy Giuliani. And it was controversial because two of the judges didn't like Rudy's politics and stormed off the stage. But they were okay with it as long as he was masked. They were okay with this masked celebrity singing contest, and you know, that's the way Israel took this Jesus of Nazareth: it's okay as long as we don't call Him the Lamb of God, it's okay as long as we don't call Him the eternal Son of God.

And we get that picture in verse 11 of John one: "He came to His own, and His own did not receive Him." And as the identity of Jesus was more and more proclaimed, more and more He's going to be rejected in His ministry.

## Who Can Take Away the Sin of the World?

Just who is this Lamb who can take away sin? Sin that brings death, ruin, destruction—who can do that?

It's the same one as in Mark chapter two, when Jesus raises up the lame man and says, "So that you may know that the Son of Man has power on earth to forgive sins"—He says to him, "Take up your bed and walk." This is the Lamb of God: the same one who has power to forgive sins. And if He can forgive sins, then He can also deliver us from death, because death is the result and the wages of sin.

That's who the Lamb is. He's the eternal Son of God as John says in verse 34. He's the one who is forever and always before time in the bosom of the Father (John 1:18). And yet He comes, born into our world, born into our human family, so that He might be the Lamb to take away the sin of the world.

He's the Lamb who has the ultimate power and is part of God's plan to not only take away sin but in taking away sin to give us life. John 1:4 says: "In Him was life and the life was the light of men." John writes again later in First John chapter one: "God is light and in Him is no darkness at all." Jesus is identified with God as God. He was the Word. The Word was with God. More than that, the Word was God, because He'd always been there as God.

## How Did John Know?

Think about the baptism here. When I was growing up, I went to a Lutheran camp and the question was asked: why do you think Jesus was baptized? Lutherans have a big focus on baptism as the washing away of sins. But Jesus is baptized also so that, at His baptism, the Holy Spirit in the form of a dove comes and rests upon Jesus. That's the picture of God Himself—God the Spirit comes and rests upon Him, on His head, on His shoulders—to say that He is the anointed one. Jesus standing in the Jordan River, the water applied to Him, and we believe that's when He begins His public ministry.

His ministry is in three offices: prophet, priest, and king. But John the Baptist is particularly focusing upon His office as the priest, because He's the one who's going to take away the sins of the world. And that's what the high priest would do. Every Yom Kippur, every Day of Atonement in the fall, the high priest would once a year offer a special sacrifice to cover the sins of the people. So Jesus is recognized in His anointing by God's Spirit, but also by His baptism, publicly beginning His priestly ministry.

Now, the description of Jesus' priesthood in Hebrews chapter seven, verses 24-27:

*"Therefore He is also able to save to the uttermost those who come to God through Him, since He always lives to make intercession for them. For such a High Priest was fitting for us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and has become higher than the heavens; who does not need daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifices, first for His own sins and then for the people's. For this He did once for all when He offered up Himself."*

There's the Lamb of God offering Himself. There's the High Priest who's been anointed to offer the sacrifice. And yet why did God even become the God-man? Because He needed a body to represent humanity, and He needed a body to offer up as a sacrifice pleasing to God. And that body was the sacrifice—His own body. When He laid down His life for us, He is the Lamb of God that takes away the sin of the world.

As God's Lamb, Jesus was without blemish. He was a man, a perfect man, to save you and me in perfect sinners. But more than a perfect man, He was God's Son—a Lamb of divine worth because He was divine. Who had always been in the bosom of the Father until He came to reveal Him for us.

## How Did John Know This Was the One?

So how did God know that Jesus was this Lamb? How did John know that Jesus would be the final sacrifice?

John says twice in this passage, "I did not know Him" (verse 31, verse 33). And yet John and Jesus were relatives. It seems like Jesus' mother Mary was some kind of relation of Elizabeth, the mother of John. We remember that story from Luke 1, when Mary came to visit Elizabeth and John as a preborn baby leaped in his mother's womb—because Mary came and brought the just-incarnate Son of God into the very same presence.

But here's how John knew. It's in verse 33, at the end: "I did not know Him, but He who sent me to baptize with water said to me, 'Upon whom you see the Spirit descending and remaining, this is He who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.'" John was given a sign. And that same Spirit that comes upon Jesus at Jesus' baptism spoke either in the mind or in the ear of John: "This is the one that you'll look for."

John baptized a lot of people down the Jordan River. But nothing like this had ever happened—to have the Spirit of God come down upon one of those baptized. And so he knew.

God can speak that way today. We don't know that God speaks audibly to His people today like that. It may happen on occasion. But we do know that the Spirit of God still does place impressions upon us. We know that from Bible teachings like 1 Corinthians 2:11-12: "No one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God. Now we have received not the spirit of the world but the Spirit who is from God"—and why? So that we might know the things that have been freely given to us by God.

God wants us to know Him. He wants us to know what He has in store for us. And one of the greatest gifts God gives us is His Word—revealing by the Spirit all the promises, all the plans of God laid out here. The Spirit of God still works in the lives of people to illumine them, especially from His Word. "I better go check on somebody. I better pray for somebody."

So God doesn't give His Spirit to everybody. 1 Corinthians 2:14: the things of God are spiritually discerned, and without that Spirit we can't know God, we just know the spirit of the world. But the Spirit of God is totally distinct from the spirit of the world.

## John the Baptist's Own Doubts in Prison

So John the Baptist said: this is the one. The Lamb of God that takes away the sin of the world. But we know this is not the end of John's relation to Jesus. Jesus actually inherits one or two disciples from John the Baptist. And John carries on, but John's going to begin to diminish and Jesus begins to advance in His public preaching and ministry—as he should.

And then the end for John the Baptist comes in a jail down by the Dead Sea. Herod the Great had built a big fortress there on the east side of the Jordan, and that's where John will die. Before he dies, he's in jail and he sends some disciples to Jesus. Matthew 11:2-3: he says, "Are you the one?"

I don't think John is so much doubting Jesus as the one—because of the great profession in John chapter one, that He's the Son of God, He's the Lamb of God. I think John is doubting himself. He had expected greater things. He thought he might set up his throne, or he thought that if Jesus has healed other people and delivered them from demonic oppression, He could deliver John from Herod. Herod's got him locked up here. So he might have been confused about what to look for.

Jesus is not upset with John when He sends a message back. He says: you don't have to tell him if I'm the one—you tell him what my ministry has been, that the lame are walking, the deaf are hearing, and the poor have the gospel preached unto them. Tell him that—and that's all it would take. Then John the Baptist could die in peace.

And so for us, Jesus gives us assurance as well. We see it through the promises. But the promises have to be mine before I draw an event from biblical promises. And then we see other things around us—other things that the Spirit of God is doing. We keep praying, and then a prayer is answered, and I didn't expect God to answer that prayer. An unexpected friendship in the time of need—just what I need, a friend, somebody calls or somebody stops by. Surprising conversions—I never expected that person to believe, but who are we to sell God short? With God nothing is impossible. God can work in any life He wants to.

Those are assurances that God gives us—that He's still keeping His word, keeping His promises, and keeping close to us.

## The Lamb in Revelation

Well, we thank God for God's grace to prepare and call a person like John the Baptist. It was a divine mercy to that generation to have a herald to go out and sound the trumpet: "It's time. Get ready to meet your King. He is the eternal Son of God. He's going to give you life. But first before He gives you life, He's going to take away the sin of the world."

And that broadens out beyond Israel. It's not just our sin but the sin of the world—to include people like us, beyond the originally chosen nation.

The apostle John has more to say about the Lamb, and we read that from Revelation chapter five. The book of Revelation focuses quite a bit on the end of the world. The message of Revelation about the end of the world is that the world is in trouble—lots of judgments, and the judgments begin to come and unfold and they get more intense. But the ancient title of the book of Revelation is the Revelation of Jesus Christ.

Right now we're waiting for the end of history. It seems like sinners have their day throughout history since Jesus came the first time. But the Bible talks about, especially in the Old Testament prophets, the Day of the Lord—and that's the book of Revelation. God, who appears to be silent sometimes, says: when I reap the harvest and gather the elect, then ultimately I will have My day, and that day is called the Day of the Lord.

So John's been caught up to heaven and he sees things beyond words. But he hears intelligible voices. And he says in verse 2: "I saw a strong angel proclaiming with a loud voice, 'Who is worthy to open the scroll and to loose its seals?'" This is a sealed scroll, and you have to have proper authority to crack those seals. This is a scroll of six to seven judgments. And as the scroll is opened, the judgments will roll out—chapter six and following.

John's observing all this and he gets engaged in it and even becomes passionate. He starts crying in verse four. And so one of the elders says in verse five: "Do not weep. Behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has prevailed to open the scroll and to loose its seven seals."

Bring in the lion! It's the Day of the Lord—now the judgments will begin to fall. So where is the lion? And so the next verse says: when He had taken the scroll, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down. And they say in verse nine: "You are worthy to take the scroll and to open its seals, for You were slain and have redeemed us to God by Your blood out of every tribe and tongue and people and nation, and have made us kings and priests to our God, and we shall reign upon the earth." And in verse twelve: "Worthy is the Lamb who was slain."

We had been looking for a lion, but it's the Lamb who is next to the throne—who stands up and comes forward and takes the scroll and says: it's time. This is the beginning of the end. The end of history as God has His day, to bring justice upon everyone who fought against Him.

And the wonderful thing is God is ransoming a people out of every tribe, tongue, and nation (verse nine). And verse ten is the positive verse—the redeemed will have a role as priest-kings, and they're going to reign upon the earth. A beautiful picture of what our salvation means.

### The Wrath of the Lamb

Let's flip over to Revelation chapter six, looking at verse fifteen. There are earthquakes and troubles in the earth, and with these earthquakes the kings of the earth, the great men, the rich men, the commanders, the mighty men, and the little people hide themselves in the caves and in the rocks of the mountains. And they said to the mountains and the rocks, "Fall on us. We're ready to die. We can't take much more of these judgments."

Why do they want the mountains to fall on them? Verse 16: to hide them from the face of Him who sits on the throne and from the **wrath of the Lamb**.

Have you ever seen an angry lamb? We might have seen an angry ram, but never an angry lamb. But here's the identity that John the Baptist introduces—and Jesus willingly and lovingly gives Himself as the sacrifice. But that sacrifice is not to be spurned or despised. And those that despised the sacrifice of Christ will suffer the consequences of turning their back upon God's Son: the wrath of the Lamb. He alone, Jesus, is the one who has the authority to judge sinners.

### Two Suppers in Revelation 19

Finally we turn to Revelation 19. This talks about the Lamb at the end. Revelation 19 is Armageddon—the final battle of the rebellion against God in history.

Are you aware that there are two suppers in chapter 19? There's the supper of the Lamb—it's a bridal supper. But then there's also another one. Look at the end of verse 17: there's been a great warfare and God calls the birds: "Come and gather together for the supper of the great God, that you may eat the flesh of kings and captains and mighty men and horses—the flesh of all people, free and slave, small and great."

Anybody fighting against God will have to deal with the wrath of the Lamb. There's the supper of the birds.

But there's another supper—and that's in verse 7 and also verse 9 of chapter 19. "Let us be glad and rejoice and give Him glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come, and His wife has made herself ready." Verse 9: "Then he said to me, 'Write: Blessed are those who are called to the marriage supper of the Lamb!' And he said to me, 'These are the true sayings of God.'"  

A blessing is pronounced upon those who are invited. And if we're invited—this is going to be a day of rejoicing. It's a day of reckoning, but it's a day of rejoicing. We're protected—God has caught us up, called us up out of this world. We're given white clothing to say we're acceptable in God's presence.

But the only ones invited to the other last supper on the battlefield are the birds. And that's one we want to avoid. Blessed are those who are invited to the marriage feast in heaven. But cursed are those who are not found at the marriage feast of the Lamb.

## Conclusion: Behold the Lamb

I draw to a conclusion. We've been talking about God's Lamb. He takes away the sin of the world. He brings life by taking away that sin. And yet for those who don't want life—and we're talking eternal life, resurrection life in God's time—they will share in the curse at the end of the age.

God gave His beloved Son as the sacrificial Lamb to take away your sin. God overcame our worst enemy when Jesus took up His life from the dead. We're talking about the resurrection of Jesus. And in defeating death, He broke the greatest tool that Satan had over us. Hebrews 2:14-15 talks about people who all their lives lived in fear of dying, fear of death. Jesus rose from the dead after the crucifixion, and He promised us that we would share in that because He's our Lamb, He's our Lord and Savior.

Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. And then in Revelation 19: behold the risen, exalted Lamb of God who one day will take away sinners out of the world. And then it'll be a better world. It'll be a renewed place. It'll be a new heaven and a new earth.

Without the Lamb who was slain, we are no better than John the Baptist wasting in prison. John was there saying, "I never thought I would die like this."—but we're all going to die. We're going to die in our beds or we may die in a prison. We are going to face death.

But with the risen Christ, with this risen Lamb who was slain, now saying, "I'm still in control and I'm taking charge of the affairs at the end of the world"—we have hope in this life. Jesus sits at the right hand of God the Father, and that knowledge gives us assurance that He ever lives to make intercession for us. So we have hope now. But we have more than that—we have an eternal life right now that gives us a future.

And it's a future not just strumming a harp, but like Revelation chapter five said, we're going to reign with Him. It's going to be an active, interesting life. But it'll be a holy life in the presence of God.

And so we add our voices to the angels of heaven, and when we consider the Lamb who takes away the sin of the world we say: **worthy is the Lamb**.

*Thank you, Lord, for revealing Yourself as our sacrifice that pleases the justice of the Father. And we pray that You'll help us to look not upon our sins and focus on that, but to focus upon the one who takes away our sins. And we pray for a right spirit in Jesus' name. Amen.*
